serving.description: 1 serving (coin prata pieces with mutton curry)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of coin prata with mutton consists of approximately 250 g, comprising several small coin-shaped prata pieces accompanied by a portion of mutton curry gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Roti Prata (Plain) and Mutton Curry — HPB Singapore Foodo / ICMR-NIN component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Coin prata with mutton is a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining HPB Foodo data for plain roti prata (approximately 60% by weight) and ICMR-NIN data for mutton curry (approximately 40% by weight). Per-100g values are weighted composites. Energy cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy for plain flour and mutton components. This is a medium-confidence estimate; no single approved source entry exists for this exact dish.

gi_source.title: Chapati / Indian flatbread GI reference — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to coin prata with mutton
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for coin prata with mutton. GI of 57 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Indian flatbread / chapati (GI ~52–62, mean ~57). The mutton curry component contributes negligible additional glycemic load due to its low carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (57 × 22.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 32.1, rounded to 32.0. gi_category set to 'medium' as GI 57 falls in the 56–69 range.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~775 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit coin prata with mutton to occasional consumption, as a single serving can contribute approximately 775 mg sodium — roughly one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and should request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~57) and moderate carbohydrate load (~56 g per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume coin prata with mutton in moderation; the medium GI of approximately 57 and a glycemic load of ~32 per serving can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise, and portion control combined with pairing with low-GI vegetables is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.1 g per 100 g) from ghee and mutton, and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake of coin prata with mutton due to its saturated fat content from ghee and mutton, which can adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels, and its moderate sodium load; choosing leaner cuts of mutton and requesting less ghee can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coin prata with mutton is a popular Singaporean-Indian dish consisting of small, coin-shaped pieces of roti prata — a flaky, layered flatbread made from plain flour, egg, ghee, and water — served alongside a rich, spiced mutton curry. The prata dough is stretched thin, folded repeatedly to create layers, then pan-fried on a flat iron griddle (tawa) with ghee or oil until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining soft and chewy inside. The mutton curry is typically prepared by slow-braising bone-in or boneless mutton pieces with onions, tomatoes, and a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, and garam masala, resulting in a deeply flavoured, moderately spiced gravy. The coin shape refers to the small, round portions of prata that are easy to tear and dip into the curry. This dish is commonly served at Indian-Muslim hawker stalls and mamak restaurants across Singapore, particularly as a breakfast or supper item. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate energy density of approximately 218 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~9.8 g) from the mutton, moderate fat (~10.2 g) including saturated fat from ghee, and carbohydrate (~22.5 g) from the flour-based prata. The sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The medium glycemic index (~57) means blood glucose response is moderate, and portion control is recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is generally halal and suitable for Muslim consumers.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for coin prata with mutton as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining HPB Foodo data for plain roti prata and ICMR-NIN data for mutton curry using a weighted proportion approach. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using Indian flatbread as the closest analogous food. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from HPB Foodo (plain roti prata component) and ICMR-NIN (mutton curry component) in an approximate 60:40 weight ratio. No single approved source covers this exact dish. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in either the HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN source entries used for this composite estimate. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN source entries used. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN source entries used. 5. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on Indian flatbread data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for coin prata with mutton was found in approved sources. gi_category is 'medium' as GI 57 falls in the 56–69 range per Veritas threshold rules. 6. Serving size of 250 g is an estimate based on typical hawker portion sizes for prata with curry in Singapore. 7. The food_group is retained as 'Meat and alternatives' consistent with the input record, reflecting the mutton component as the primary protein source. 8. Halal status is flagged as true based on the typical preparation at Indian-Muslim hawker stalls; individual restaurant verification is recommended.
